creator: koitsu@FreeBSD.org
text:
The first point you make is fair enough; I absolutely cannot argue with
that, and will not.  If you don't want the responsibility, or the
headaches involved, that's cool.

I did not check the PR database for previously rejected requests,
although I will do that momentarily for my own self-education.  Thank
you for pointing me in that direction.

Adding other channels should be as easy as using saupdate_flags (for
multiple channels, --channel arguments should suffice; sa-update(1) even
documents that).

Regarding GPG failing, I have to assume that upon a key failure
sa-update will exit with an exit code of 4 or greater.  I would have to
go look at the perl code to actually see if that's the case.  If it
doesn't (e.g. blindly bombs out), that's a problem with sa-update and
not a problem with the periodic script, and would have to be reported
upstream.  I can see how this might cause you to get mails or PRs from
users asking for help, although the periodic script *does* default to
being disabled (saupdate_enable="no"), so that would limit the userbase
nagging you.

GPG keys not being updated would be the administrators' responsibility.
sa-update should output some nastiness to stdout in such a condition,
which would hopefully clue the administrator in.  I'll use Apache as a
counterpoint -- when your SSL certs expire, a periodic log rolling
script isn't to be held responsible for it when it induces rc.d/apache22
stop/start.  I see your point, but I think it's far-fetched.

Finally, sa-compile support could be added to the periodic script, but
I'll remind you that sa-compile is *optional*.  Another conditional
variable (yes/no) wouldn't hurt.

Thanks for providing a more verbose explanation like I requested.  I'll
close this PR per your request, predominantly because of your first
point.

-- 
| Jeremy Chadwick                                jdc at parodius.com |
